With respect to an apparatus for preventing an overheating of the compressor by introducing a high pressure liquefied coolant into the compression chamber under the compression stroke within the compressor in a refrigeration cycle formed by the prescribed displacement type compressor, no consideration has been given to the communicating position of the connecting pipe for introducing the high pressure liquefied coolant during the compression stroke. In the fixed displacement type compressor, in the compression stroke, the compression is forcibly performed up to a prescribed pressure ratio determined in dependence upon the prescribed volume ratio and the coolant to be used, and thereafter, the condensation pressure is obtained by communicating the coolant to the discharge space. [2] RelevRelevant links: [1] https://lwn.net/Articles/345679/ [2] https://www.kernel.org/doc/html/latest/networking/gro.html To elaborate on the differences and the relationship between Generic Receive Offload (GRO) and Large Receive Offloading (LRO): - Large Receive Offloading (LRO): This is a hardware-based technique that combines multiple small incoming packets into larger ones before passing them up to the network stack. LRO reduces CPU usage by decreasing the number of interrupts and system calls required to process individual packets, but it has limitations in terms of compatibility with non-TCP traffic and bridged networks. - Generic Receive Offload (GRO): GRO is a software-based mechanism that performs similar functions to LRO but does so more flexibly. It can handle a wider range of protocols beyond TCP and works well with bridging, making it more versatile than LRO. Additionally, while GRO operates in the kernel's networking stack, drivers can still leverage hardware offloading capabilities where available. In summary, while both techniques aim to improve network performance by reducing CPU overhead, GRO is designed to be a more general solution

texts.full_text: The first laboratory experiments on liver transplantation were performed with auxiliary heterotopic grafts. The initial clinical results of HLT, however, were disappointing and orthotopic liver transplantation (OLT) evolved to be the procedure of choice. Of all the patientswho underwent OLT during that period, a significant number experienced complications related to the removal of their native livers or had underlying diseases such as hepatitis B where the risk of reinfection in an immunosuppressed patient was high; these challenges led researchers back to exploring heterotopic liver transplantation (HLT) with renewed interest. The HLT technique involves transplanting a donor liver into a recipient without removing the diseased organ, thereby providing several theoretical advantages including avoiding potential complications associated with native hepatectomy and offering an immunological advantage by possibly inducing tolerance through mixed chimerism between host and graft immune cells. Recent advances in surgical techniques and improvements in post-operative care have sparked renewed interest in HLT as a viable option for patients suffering from end-stage liver disease, particularly those where preserving the original organ is desirable or necessary due to its potential residual function that could support patient recovery durinG periods of rejection crisis without necessitating immediate re-transplantation. texts.full_text: (motorneurons) responsible for breathing and all movement. Harvard researchers have found a compound that stabilized this protein in mouse and human motor neurons. This may lead to the development of newtreatments for motor neuron diseases including Spinal Muscular Atrophy andLou Gehrig’s disease. Scientists from the Harvard Stem Cell Institutehave identified a compound that helps protect the cells destroyed by spinal muscular atrophy (SMA), the most frequent fatal genetic disease in children under 2 years of age. Spinal muscular atrophyis a neurodegenerative disease targeting motor neurons, the long nerve cells that relay messages from the brain to the muscles.It is caused by a mutation in the SMN1 gene,which is crucial for the survival of motor neurons. Without the SMN protein produced by this gene, these neurons die, leading to muscle weakness and atrophy. The Harvard researchers, led by Professor Chris Lengner, discovered a compound called SMN-stabilizing drug (SMS), which can stabilize the SMN protein in the neurons.In a study published in the journal "Nature Communications," they reported that SMS significantly extended the lifespan ofmice with SMA, restored their motor function, and increased the number of motorneurons in their spinal cords. Furthermore, the researchers found that SMS also stabilized the SMN protein in human motor neurons derived from induced pluripotent stem cells (iPSCs). This suggests that SMS could potentially be effective in treating not only SMA but also other motor neuron diseases,such as amyotrophic lateral sclerosis (ALS), commonly known as Lou Gehrig's disease. ALS is a progressive neurodegenerative disease that affects motor neuronsin the brainand the spinal cord, leading to muscle weaknessand paralysis. Like SMA, ALS is also characterized by the loss of motorneurons, and the SMN protein is implicated in the disease process. The discovery of SMS as a potential treatment for both SMA and ALSrepresents a significant step forward in the fight against these devastating diseases. Currently, most kidney disorders are diagnosed by measuring kidney function, for example by using biochemical tests such as assays that measure serum creatinine (Cr) concentrations, and by imaging or biopsy. Presently, renal biopsy remains the most definitive test to specifically diagnose many kidney disorders. However, renal biopsy has major limitations. The project will cost $1,48 billion and is expected to deliver an additional 600 megawatts; 300W from each unit, on top of the average of 450MW the old power station is currently able to generate. texts.full_text: to teach the quintessentially nationalistic course in American history in an era in which a great deal of the historiography is transnational or at least comparative, although that is a challenge for me considering the way I teach the rest of my courses. It’s really the overwhelmingly nationalistic, solipsistic, chest-beating, flag-waving, screaching bald eagle totality of the historiography. In the United States at least, there is no more nationalistic course, and no course that is taught in such a one-sided, pro-American manner. And the students love it! They demand it, in fact, and they revel in the opportunity to indulge in nationalist agitprop in their essays. Every course I ever took on the American Revolution–from unreconstructed Whiggy consensus historians to the leftiest of the New Lefties–was unanimous in its judgment that the Americans were right to seek independence from Britain, and American military and diplomatic victories were cheered unresevedly. This is not surprising given what we now know about how many people fought for one side or another during this period. The debate between Braaten's modern reinterpretation of justification through faith in Christ versus Stendahl-Dunn’s historical perspective has prompted a reexamination on how to best interpret Pauline texts. This challenge remains central for theological reflection today, with no clear resolution yet. Wright and NPP indeed offer fresh insights into understanding early Christian thought but at times may appear more interested about addressing contemporary concerns rather than deeply engaging in an accurate historical reconstruction of first-century Jewish Christianity. texts.full_text: t came in Don Siegel's The Gun Runners, an adaptation of Ernest Hemingway's To Have and Have Not. She appeared with Sullivan in the 1958 movie Wolf Larsen. In 2013, Hall settled a lawsuit she filed against Lionsgate, the production company behind the TV series Mad Men for using an image of her gleaned from a 1960s Revlon ad in the show's opening title credits sequence. The image was taken from a Richard Avedon photograph; the producers had secured permission from Revlon, but not from Hall. Personal life Hall married Leslie Jackson in August 1954. After they divorced, she married Sullivan in 1958; they divorced in 1961. Hall was the mother of Sullivan's daughter Birgitta ‘Patricia’ Christina. Known as Patsy, Gita’s daughter by Sullivan, followed in her footsteps and became a top model and cover girl as well, starting when she was 12. Hall later married Mitchell May III, an insurance broker and had two more daughters, Tracie and Samantha with May. He died in 2000. In 2003, Gita moved from Manhattan to Century City, California to go back to acting. Hall had a short lived reality show called ‘ Old Skool’ co-starring with actress Terry Moore. The show found an audience in Europe but only had a year run. Bunim Murray was the producer and distributor. Hall also appeared in on line commercials and had small parts in several network television shows. Hall was dressed to the nines in designer clothing and matched her dress for the day to fine jewelry she collected. Her daughters say, ‘Mom was the Swedish Joan Collins, she was very fancy and also quick to laugh. She was fabulous.’ Death Hall died of liver failure in Studio City, Los Angeles. She was survived by three daughters, nine grandchildren and one great-granddaughter. texts.full_text: NOWAIT statement, which returns rows R1 and R2, is run from concurrent connections S1 and S2. S1 obtains and locks R1. S2 obtains and locks R2. S1 tries to obtain R2, but locked by S2. S2 tries to obtain R1, but it's locked by S1. The lock on R1 is released due to S1 failing. The question is whether step 4 can actually happen between step 3 and 5, or else steps 3 and 5 are performed atomically with respect to concurrent selects. I'm guessing it cannot happen, since without NOWAIT (or SKIP LOCKED), this behavior would lead to deadlock (S1 waits for S2 to finish and release R2 while S2 waits for S1 to finish and release R1), but maybe such scenario would be resolved in some other way. So, which are the guarantees here? A: In your scenario step 3 should fail, if it tries to obtain R2 with NOWAIT option. When it fails all locks of S1 are released immediately, which would prevent the scenario you described in step 4. When you use the NOWAIT option in your SELECT statement, it attempts to obtain the locks on the rows it needs. If it fails to obtain all the locks it needs, it will not proceed and will instead release all the locks it has acquired and will report an error. This behavior ensures that the locks are not held in an inconsistent state and prevents the deadlock situation that you are concerned about. So, in the sequence you described, the following would happen: 1. S1 locks R1. 2. S2 locks R2. 3. S1 tries to lock R2, but R2 is already locked by S2. Since

texts.full_text: on the Illumina HumanMethylation450 BeadChip platform. Unsupervised clustering analysis using the most variable probes (top 1%) in the 48 RMS tumors revealed that DNA methylation patterns in these tumors segregated into two distinct clusters; one cluster contains all 21 FP cases along with 2 FN cases and a second cluster contains 25 of the 27 FN cases. This study thus confirms our previous findings in a separate cohort of 37 RMS cases analyzed with the Infinium HumanMethylation27 BeadChip platform. Beyond the initial separation of FP and FN cases, our current DNA methylation studies with the 48 RMS case cohort identified two major subgroups within the FP cluster and two major subgroups within the FN cluster. To further examine the significance of these novel methylation-defined subgroups, we compared the DNA methylation profiles in this new cohort of 48 RMS tumors with mutation, gene fusion, and copy number data for these cases. In my humble opinion, here are five actors who exemplified excellence in 2017: 1. Frances McDormand (Three Billboards Outside Ebbing, Missouri): She embodies a wide spectrum of emotions with her portrayal of Mildred—passionate yet mired in grief and anger at the perceived injustice that her daughter suffered without recourse to justice. 2. Octavia Spencer (Hidden Figures): As Katherine Johnson, she masterfully represents intelligence and resilience against the backdrop of early civil rights struggles in America—a journey that is at once personal and universally significant. 4. Luke Kleintank: His dual roles as both a spirited stripper in "The Florida Project" and an unflinchingly tough guy in "La La Land," showcase his range, versatility, and ability to make even the most challenging of characters believable and captivating on-screen. 5. texts.full_text: explain to the common man what is happening, but it is fundamental to try. For the last 50 years the CAP has taken over almost half of 73 billion euros from European taxpayers each year and given them back in various forms as subsidies for agriculture: direct payments (which are distributed according to how much land you have), aid per head based on animal stock, rural development support funds or other types. In this way a large part the food we eat is paid by our taxes. The idea behind all of these was that it would be possible to guarantee stable incomes for farmers and at the same time keep prices low enough so as not to burden consumers too much with their tax payments; but in reality things have turned out differently. The European market has become a dumping ground where food from anywhere is allowed, without controls or guarantees on its quality. This system of subsidies was designed by people who wanted Europe’s agriculture based around industrial monoculture and chemical products: it does not work for those small-scale farmers whose goal is to produce high-quality foods that are good for the environment. This means there have been no incentives in place over time, at least until now when a new CAP should be born. texts.full_text: discuss their dreams. It involves the physical locations where my dreams take place. I rarely dream about any real place, and almost all of my dreams occur in places that don’t exist in real life, or at least don’t exist as I see them. What’s interesting is that, although the dreams themselves never repeat verbatim, many of the locales do. Some of them I’ve been “visiting” in my subconscious for literally years. Furthermore, the locations change gradually over time. I can see some of them so vividly that in some cases I’m able to draw maps and diagrams of the locations. Exploring these dream locations, a little bit at a time, is truly one of the coolest things that my very active mind has ever done. Far and away, the most well-explored of my dream locations, and probably the most interesting, is what I call “The School.” I can’t remember exactly when I first recall dreaming about this place, but it was certainly no later than 1998 or 1999. I call it “The School” because that’s what it was–at least at first. It resembled the high school I attended in the Philadelphia area in the late 1980s (which has, obliquely, made an appearance on this blog before), but with a number of strange modifications. In real life the high school was built in the 1950s and had not been remodeled when I went there more than 30 years later, so it definitely had a “retro” look. In the dreams I’ve seen large corridors filled with blue and orange metal

texts.full_text: Couple get prison for role in tax evasion Friday Nov 30, 2012 at 6:00 AM A Rhode Island husband and wife were sentenced this week to prison for their role in a tax evasion scheme that involved New Hampshire income tax resisters William S. Dion and Catherine Floyd, both formerly of Uxbridge. U.S. District Judge Dennis F. Saylor in Boston sentenced Gail Thorick to seven months in prison followed by 17 months of supervised release with seven months of that time to be served in home confinement. Her husband, Myron Thorick, received two years’ probation, with the first year to be served in home confinement. Judge Saylor also ordered the Thoricks to pay a total of $143,000 in restitution to the state of New Hampshire.

texts.full_text: and their objects' names. Unfortunately, info panel nor python hints have not been helpful to find the command. How do I get a list of all collections' names? How do I get a list of all objects' names in a given collection? Thank you for your time! A: You can access all collection Through bpy.data.collections. The objects within the collection (and child collections) are given by bpy.data.collections.all_objects. This is documented in the BlendData types and the documentation of the collection type. A little example script for printing the content of the collection``` import bpy # Get all collections collections = bpy.data.collections # Print each collection name and its objects' names for coll in collections: print(f"Collection: {coll.name}") # Iterate over objects within this collection (and child collections) for obj in coll.all_objects: print(f"- Object: {obj.name}") ```
